# LegalSum — B2B SaaS Launch Audit
**Product:** AI-powered legal document summarization  
**ICP:** Mid-size law firms  
**Model:** $49/month flat rate, GPT-4 at $0.03/req, ~50 req/user/month

---

```

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Ask your 3 most enthusiastic beta contacts to pay a founding
    member rate (e.g., $29/month locked in) before launch day.

[FAIL] Problem validated (not just interest)
  → Next step: Document 10+ specific instances where a lawyer lost billable time
    or made an error because they lacked fast document summaries — this is your
    validation evidence.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested
  → Next step: Run 5 real document summarization workflows manually (human in
    the loop) to confirm the AI output is good enough for legal use before
    trusting the automated pipeline.
```

---

**Calculated context:** Single flat tier at $49/month. No usage tiers or annual incentive mentioned.

```
[FAIL] Value metric clearly identified and tied to pricing
  → Next step: Define your value metric explicitly — "per summary" or "per
    matter" aligns billing with lawyer outcomes better than a flat seat fee.
    Update pricing page copy to reflect this.

[FAIL] 3–4 tiers with clear differentiation
  → Next step: Design at least 3 tiers. For law firms: Solo ($29),

# SaaS Launch Audit Report
**Product:** LegalSum — AI-powered document summarization for legal teams
**ICP:** Mid-size law firms
**Stage:** Ready to Launch
**Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## Pre-Audit Notes

**AI Economics (pre-calculated):**
- Cost per user/month: 50 requests × $0.03 = **$1.50**
- Revenue per user/month: **$49.00**
- AI cost as % of revenue: **3.1%** (well under the 30% threshold)
- Gross margin (AI-adjusted): **~96.9%**

These numbers are strong. The unit economics section will be the brightest spot in this report.

---

## Section 1 — Pre-Launch Validation

```
[FAIL] 50+ discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Pause launch prep and conduct at least 20-30 structured discovery
    calls with attorneys or legal ops managers at mid-size firms. Focus on where
    document review currently breaks down, not on your product.

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Offer a founding-member rate ($29–$39/month, locked forever) to
    your warmest 5 prospects and require a credit card before granting access.
    Pre-payment is the only signal that validates real demand.

[FAIL] Problem validated (not just interest)
  → Next step: Document 3+ specific instances where a prospect described a
    concrete cost or pain (e.g., "associates spend 4 hours reviewing NDAs before
    every M&A intake"). Vague interest ("sounds useful") does not count.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested
  → Next step: Deliver the summarization service manually (using the same GPT-4
    prompts) for at least 2 real client documents from a pilot firm before
    automating. Validates output quality under real legal document conditions.
```

**Section 1 result: 0/4 PASS**

---

## Section 2 — Pricing Strategy

```
[FAIL] Pricing model is outcome- or usage-based (not per-seat flat rate only)
  → Next step: Reconsider the $49/month flat rate. Law firms strongly prefer
    predictable pricing

```
[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested
```

# Launch Readiness Audit — B2C Consumer Fitness App

**Profile:** B2C · Casual gym-goers 25-40 · Beta → Public Launch · AI workout recs ($0.01/req, 20 req/user/mo) · Freemium + $9.99/mo premium · URL: placeholder

```
    current apps, and what would make them pay; document verbatim quotes as evidence.

[FAIL] Pre-payment or strong intent secured from early users
  → Next step: Offer beta users a "founding member" deal at $4.99/mo locked forever — real
    payment intent (even a waitlist deposit or pre-order) is the only true validation signal.

[FAIL] Problem validated beyond stated interest
  → Next step: Identify whether beta users return week-over-week without prompting; track 
    D7 and D30 retention — if < 20% return after week 1, the problem isn't painful enough yet.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested
  → Next step: Run a "Wizard of Oz" week — deliver AI-generated workout plans manually via 
    DM to 10 beta users before trusting the automated system is solving the real problem.
```

```
    not the feature, is the value metric.

[FAIL] 3-4 tiers with clear differentiation

# SaaS Launch Audit — B2C Fitness App
**Product:** AI-powered workout recommendations
**ICP:** Casual gym-goers, 25–40
**Stage:** Beta → Public Launch
**Pricing:** Freemium + $9.99/mo premium

**URL:** Placeholder (not live)

---

```
[FAIL] 50+ discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Conduct structured interviews with 50 casual gym-goers before launch;
    focus on why they stop going to the gym, not what app features they want.

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Offer a "founding member" deal ($79/year lifetime) to beta users now;
    even 3 paid signups validate willingness-to-pay before you invest in launch.

[FAIL] Problem validated (not just interest)
  → Next step: Document 5 specific quotes from beta users describing a concrete problem
    the app solved (e.g., "I didn't know what to do on leg day") — interest ≠ validation.

[PASS] Manual service delivery tested
  → Beta stage confirms the core loop has been exercised by real users.
```

**Section Score: 1/4 PASS**

---

## Section 2 — Pricing Strategy

```
[FAIL] Pricing model is outcome/usage-based (not just flat subscription)
  → Next step: Add a usage-based dimension — e.g., free tier gets 5 AI recommendations/month,
    premium gets unlimited. This creates a natural upgrade trigger tied to value delivery.

[FAIL] Value metric clearly identified
  → Next step: Define your value metric explicitly. For a fitness app, the best candidates
    are "workouts completed" or "AI recommendations consumed" — pick one and build
    your tier logic around it.

[FAIL] 3–4 tiers with clear differentiation
  → Next step: Add a third tier (e.g., "Coach" at $19.99/mo) with features like
    progress analytics, custom plans, or trainer access — two tiers leaves money on the table.

[FAIL] Annual
